The process is reversible, which means that ATP may be re-formed from adenosine diphosphate (ADP) as long as there is sufficient energy to restore the missing phosphate molecule on to the ADP. The latter can be achieved by phosphocreatine (PCr) or by processes such as anaerobic glycolysis, and aerobic processes.

The stores of ATP in muscle tissue are rather limited, so there is a constant need to resynthesize it for survival, let alone movement. The amount of ATP in a muscle cell amounts to 25 mM/kg dry muscle or about 40–50 g in total, which is sufficient to enable high intense activity for around 2–4 seconds if it is the only useable source of energy available. This is not a great amount – hence the importance of resynthesis of ATP at rates sufficient to enable appropriate levels of exercise to ensue, i.e. fast rates of resynthesis for sprinting and slower rates for prolonged exercise.

## Definition

ATP resynthesis is the reversible re-formation of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) from adenosine diphosphate (ADP) by restoring a phosphate group onto ADP, provided that sufficient energy is available.

## Core Principles

- The ATP ⇌ ADP reaction is reversible: ATP can be re-formed from ADP whenever sufficient energy is available to reattach the missing phosphate group.
- Energy for restoring the phosphate onto ADP can be supplied by phosphocreatine (PCr), anaerobic glycolysis, and aerobic (oxidative) processes.
- Muscle ATP stores are limited (~25 mM/kg dry muscle, or ~40–50 g total), sufficient for only ~2–4 seconds of high-intensity activity if used as the sole energy source, making continuous resynthesis essential.
- The required rate of ATP resynthesis is matched to exercise demands: fast rates support sprinting, slower rates support prolonged exercise.
- Phosphorus-containing metabolites involved in ATP turnover include ATP, ADP, adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and inorganic phosphate (P*i*), alongside intramuscular pH changes that accompany resynthesis pathway activity.
- Muscle cells possess a highly coordinated and regulatory network of signalling and feedback pathways which function to ensure ATP demand is matched by ATP synthesis.
- The enzymes governing ATP-resynthesis pathways are regulated acutely by covalent modification (phosphorylation/dephosphorylation), allosteric regulation by contraction-derived signalling molecules (ADP, AMP, IMP, P*i*, Ca2+, H+, etc.), and by substrate activation / product inhibition; longer-term regulation occurs via increases in enzyme protein content (e.g. with endurance training).

## Sub-concepts

- Phosphocreatine (PCr) system as a rapid phosphate donor for ADP rephosphorylation.
- Anaerobic glycolysis as an oxygen-independent pathway contributing to ATP resynthesis.
- Aerobic (oxidative) metabolism as the sustained pathway for ATP resynthesis.
- Methodological approaches to studying ATP resynthesis: muscle biopsy and blood sampling during cycling, running and maximal voluntary contractions (MVCs); magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) for 'real time' measurement of phosphorus-containing metabolites (ATP, ADP, AMP, P*i*) and pH, primarily during isometric contractions.
- Physiological modulators of substrate utilization (and therefore of which resynthesis pathway predominates) during endurance exercise: exercise intensity, exercise duration, nutritional status, and training status.

## Practical Applications

- Because endogenous ATP stores only fuel ~2–4 seconds of high-intensity work, training and performance strategies depend on engaging the appropriate resynthesis pathway: PCr and anaerobic glycolysis for sprint/short high-intensity efforts, and aerobic metabolism for prolonged exercise.
- Interpreting ATP resynthesis data from MRS or from biopsies following MVC should be done with caution when applying findings to dynamic high-intensity exercise (HIE), since these techniques predominantly capture isometric or static contraction conditions; nonetheless, MVC plus biopsy approaches (e.g. Hultman *et al*., 1981) have yielded valuable metabolite data.
- When matching training prescription to a desired metabolic adaptation, manipulate exercise intensity, duration, nutritional status and training status, recognising that these factors act through the acute (covalent/allosteric/substrate-product) and chronic (enzyme content) regulatory control points of the resynthesis pathways.
